entries.title: ToolHive MCP SSRF: host-side discovery runs outside the sandbox it enforces
entries.summary: ToolHive isolates every MCP server in a container, but its remote-auth discovery code runs host-side, outside that sandbox, and follows a server-controlled `WWW-Authenticate: resource_metadata` URL (and its redirects) with no private-IP guard. A malicious remote MCP server can 302 the host to 169.254.169.254; where AWS IMDSv1 is enabled that returns IAM credentials directly, and on IMDSv2/GCP the reachable impact is the broader internal-GET surface — either way the exact reach the container sandbox exists to deny.

lessons.point: A security control that runs outside the boundary it protects doesn't count: the discovery code executes in the host process, before the per-server container sandbox.
lessons.excerpt: This discovery code runs host-side, in the ToolHive process, before and outside that per-server container sandbox.

lessons.point: The SSRF was actively waved through: maintainers added `#nosec G704` suppressions asserting the URL was 'internal config', a premise that contradicts the project's own 'MCP server is untrusted' threat model.
lessons.excerpt: The maintainers saw the taint-analysis warning on these requests and waved it through on a trust assumption that contradicts the project's design.

lessons.point: Validating only the initial URL is not enough — the redirect target must be re-validated, because an HTTPS metadata URL can 302 to an internal http address.
lessons.excerpt: Re-validating the redirect target, not only the initial URL, is the load-bearing part

entries.threat: A remote MCP server the victim intentionally connects to can drive the host to issue arbitrary internal GETs and, on AWS IMDSv1, exfiltrate IAM credentials.
entries.conditions: User adds/runs a malicious or compromised remote MCP server via the normal `thv run --remote-auth` workflow; no user targeting of an internal address needed.
entries.mitigations: Wrap discovery clients with the existing IsPrivateIP dialer guard, set CheckRedirect to reject cross-host/scheme-downgrade redirects, and re-validate every redirect hop — not just the first URL.
entries.takeaway: Put SSRF guards on every outbound client that touches untrusted input, re-validate redirect targets, and never suppress a taint warning on a 'trusted config' premise your threat model calls untrusted.

actionable.title: SSRF-guard every host-side fetch in an agent/MCP runtime
actionable.detail: For any outbound request whose URL derives from a tool/server response, enforce a private-IP dialer deny-list AND a CheckRedirect that re-applies it to each hop; treat `#nosec`/lint suppressions on such sinks as findings to review against the threat model, not settled decisions.

entries.prior_art: Classic cloud-metadata SSRF (169.254.169.254) is well known; the novel delta is the MCP-specific vector — a control running host-side, outside the very container sandbox the tool advertises, reached purely through a server's auth-discovery response.
entries.verify_note: Independent verifier: lessons supported; qualified IAM-cred read to AWS IMDSv1 (IMDSv2/GCP = internal-GET surface).

entries.title: OpenClaw's ClawHub skill marketplace: an agentic supply-chain attack surface
entries.summary: Unit 42 found five evasive malicious 'skills' persisting on ClawHub — the marketplace OpenClaw's agent pulls third-party, markdown-driven packages from with broad local system access — despite VirusTotal + ClawScan screening added after February's incidents. The five span infostealers (macOS, C2), scanner evasion (inflated file size to exceed thresholds), and two NOVEL agentic threats: runtime agentic affiliate injection and agentic front-running.
entries.takeaway: Agent skill marketplaces are a critical, largely-untrusted link in the software supply chain — marketplace scanning alone does not make them safe.

lessons.point: Agent skills are broad-access packages; the marketplace is a supply-chain link to treat as untrusted
lessons.excerpt: markdown-driven packages with broad local system access, making ClawHub a critical link in the agentic software supply chain

lessons.point: Malware evades marketplace scanners with simple tricks (oversized files exceed scan thresholds)
lessons.excerpt: inflated file size to exceed scanner thresholds, bypassing both ClawScan and VirusTotal detection

lessons.point: New 'agentic' threat classes are emerging — affiliate injection and front-running done at agent runtime
lessons.excerpt: runtime agentic affiliate injection and agentic front-running. Both are novel techniques

actionable.title: Vet and sandbox agent skills
actionable.detail: Pin and review third-party agent skills from trusted publishers, sandbox their execution with least-privilege, and don't rely on marketplace scanning — add your own allow-list + runtime policy.

entries.title: ShadowPickle: pickle-VM import tricks evade ten model scanners and four model hubs
entries.author: Pradhan, Nambiar, Soremekun
entries.summary: ShadowPickle presents three stealthy pickle-deserialization attacks that abuse the Pickle VM's external module import mechanism to execute payloads at load time while evading scanners. The reported evaluation evades ten state-of-the-art scanners and four model hubs, with the 'Overwritten' variant reaching a 63% evasion rate.

lessons.point: The pickle VM's external module import mechanism is the bypass primitive; evading ten scanners and four hubs is consistent with - though the abstract does not itself argue - hub scanning being weak evidence of artifact safety.
lessons.excerpt: These attacks leverage the external module import mechanism of the Pickle Virtual Machine (VM) to execute malicious payloads during deserialization.

lessons.point: Evasion is measured, not asserted: the 'Overwritten' variant reports a 63% aggregate evasion rate across the scanner suite (the abstract gives no per-scanner breakdown), so treat scanner coverage as probabilistic, not a gate.
lessons.excerpt: SHADOWPICKLE (Overwritten) has a 63% evasion rate across scanners, and up to 50% higher evasion rates than existing attacks.

lessons.point: The authors describe PickleBench, a harness that injects these attacks into arbitrary benign models; the abstract does not state whether it is publicly released.
lessons.excerpt: we provide PICKLEBENCH, a dynamic and extensible benchmark for automatically injecting SHADOWPICKLE into arbitrary benign PTM models

entries.threat: An attacker publishes a pre-trained model to a hub whose pickle payload executes on deserialization in the consumer's environment while passing hub and third-party scanners.
entries.conditions: Any pipeline that loads pickle-format pre-trained models from a hub and treats a scanner verdict as sufficient assurance.
entries.mitigations: The abstract says the authors give security recommendations but does not enumerate them. Independent of the paper, standard practice applies: prefer non-executable formats such as safetensors and deserialize untrusted models only in a sandbox.
entries.takeaway: A clean model-scanner result is weak evidence - prefer non-executable formats and sandbox deserialization of any third-party model.

actionable.title: Regression-test your model scanner with PickleBench
actionable.detail: If PickleBench is released, run your model-scanning gate against injected variants of your own known-good models to measure real evasion rate before relying on it in CI. Independent of the paper: where the format allows, migrate ingestion to safetensors and make pickle loading an explicitly sandboxed, opt-in path.

entries.prior_art: Malicious pickles in ML model hubs are a well-established attack class (pickle RCE documented since at least 2022, incl. Rehberger's ML Attack Series). The delta is the specific external-module-import evasion primitive plus a measured evasion rate across ten scanners and an injectable benchmark, rather than a new concept.
entries.verify_note: Independent verifier: 63% is an aggregate evasion rate, not 'a majority of scanners'; dropped unsupported 'strongest variant' and public-release claims; safetensors advice re-attributed as ours, not the paper's. Abstract-only source.

entries.title: Agent skill security is a lifecycle problem, not just a runtime one (SkillSec-Eval)
entries.summary: Reusable agent 'skills' are a new supply-chain unit, but security work has mostly looked at prompt injection and runtime execution. SkillSec-Eval maps threats across the whole skill lifecycle — repository admission, semantic retrieval, planner selection, execution, and skill evolution — and, over 327 real skills, shows vulnerabilities appear at stages well before execution.

lessons.point: Skill security today is scoped too narrowly — it fixates on prompt injection and runtime, leaving the rest of the skill lifecycle unexamined.
lessons.excerpt: existing security research primarily focuses on prompt injection and runtime execution, leaving security risks throughout the broader skill lifecycle largely unexplored

lessons.point: A useful threat model spans five lifecycle stages, so a skill scanner should check admission, retrieval, and planner-selection risks, not only execution.
lessons.excerpt: a threat taxonomy spanning repository admission, semantic retrieval, planner selection, execution, and skill evolution

lessons.point: Empirically, across 327 real-world skills, vulnerabilities show up at multiple stages beyond execution — so lifecycle-aware analysis is needed, not just a runtime guard.
lessons.excerpt: vulnerabilities arise at multiple lifecycle stages beyond execution

entries.takeaway: When you scan or admit agent skills, cover the whole lifecycle (admission, retrieval, planner selection, evolution) — a runtime-only check misses where most of the risk actually lives.

actionable.title: Lifecycle-aware skill scanning
actionable.detail: Extend a skill/marketplace scanner beyond runtime: check repository-admission provenance, semantic-retrieval poisoning (does a crafted description win retrieval?), planner-selection hijacking, and skill-evolution drift between approved and current versions.

entries.prior_art: OpenClaw/ClawHub agentic supply-chain and MCP tool-poisoning work cover parts of this; the novel delta is the explicit lifecycle taxonomy (five stages) plus an empirical study over 327 real skills rather than a single attack demo.
entries.verify_note: Independent verifier: all three lessons match the abstract; no fabricated attack/defense specifics.

entries.title: Provider safety guardrails blocked incident response during the Hugging Face agentic intrusion
entries.author: Johann Rehberger
entries.summary: Hugging Face disclosed an intrusion it says was driven end to end by an autonomous AI agent. By its own account a malicious dataset gave code execution, after which the agent took host access, harvested cloud/cluster credentials and moved laterally, logging 17,000+ events; HF did not publish exploit details or IOCs, and none of it is independently corroborated. The sharpest lesson is the response: HF says it first tried frontier models behind commercial APIs and provider safety guardrails repeatedly blocked the forensic workload, forcing a pivot to a locally hosted open-weight model.

lessons.point: Vendor safety guardrails blocked forensic analysis of attacker payloads and commands via commercial APIs mid-incident, forcing a pivot to a local open-weight model - treat provider refusal as an IR availability risk.
lessons.excerpt: initially tried to use frontier models behind commercial APIs, but provider safety guardrails repeatedly blocked the forensic workload

lessons.point: Stage a locally deployable open-weight model as a forensic fallback BEFORE an incident - procuring one under fire is not a plan. Local execution also keeps attacker data and credentials inside your environment.
lessons.excerpt: it is probably a good idea to vet and stage a locally deployable open-weight model before an incident occurs, not during one

lessons.point: Agentic intrusions reuse familiar TTPs - the delta defenders must absorb is velocity and scale, not novel exploitation.
lessons.excerpt: the exploits and TTPs will be quite familiar at first, but the velocity and scale of agentic adversaries is something defenders need to adapt to

entries.threat: An autonomous agent framework establishes a beachhead via a malicious dataset, escalates to node-level access, harvests cloud and cluster credentials, and moves laterally at machine speed.
entries.conditions: A model/dataset hub that executes remote dataset-loader code and templates from untrusted uploads; defenders relying on commercial model APIs for forensic analysis. Note: the intrusion narrative is Hugging Face's own account; exploit details and IOCs were not published.
entries.mitigations: Vet and pre-stage a self-hosted open-weight model for forensics; keep an LLM-based anomaly triager (HF's flagged the intrusion first); rotate tokens and review account activity.
entries.takeaway: Assume your commercial AI provider may refuse to help you during a breach - pre-stage a local open-weight forensic model the same way you pre-stage backups.

actionable.title: Pre-stage a local open-weight model as forensic fallback
actionable.detail: Add to the IR runbook: identify, license-check and stage a self-hosted open-weight model capable of analyzing raw attacker payloads/logs, and test it on a red-team sample before an incident. Treat commercial-API refusal as an expected failure mode, not an edge case, and raise it with your enterprise account team.

entries.prior_art: Guardrails obstructing legitimate security and malware analysis is a long-running, frequently-reported community complaint, and the intrusion TTPs themselves are conventional. The delta is a fresh, concrete, named instance - a vendor pivoting to a self-hosted open-weight model mid-incident - not a new phenomenon.
entries.verify_note: Independent verifier: removed an invented causal claim (that exploit-gen and forensic refusals are the same mechanism); hedged the intrusion narrative as HF's own uncorroborated account; novelty 74->45 (guardrails-block-security-work is a known complaint).

entries.title: Insecure coding preferences persist in LLM long-term memory and resist normal correction
entries.author: Yuchen Chen et al.
entries.summary: Across four LLMs (ChatGPT, Gemini, Qwen, Grok) and five languages, storing insecure coding preferences in long-term memory raised vulnerable-code generation by 2.7-50.3 percentage points, and those memories proved hard to overwrite through ordinary conversation. Warning rates lagged vulnerability rates by 5.4-14.0 pp, so the model gets less safe faster than it gets more cautious - and only memory-level filtering restored baseline behavior. Scope: this is LLM-based code generation with persistent memory, not autonomous tool-using agents.

lessons.point: Long-term memory is a persistent, cross-session attack surface: once an insecure preference is stored it silently steers later security-critical generations.
lessons.excerpt: once insecure coding preferences are stored, they may silently influence security-critical decisions in subsequent generations

lessons.point: You cannot reliably talk a poisoned memory out of the system - the effect survives rephrasing and normal interaction.
lessons.excerpt: insecure memories are difficult to overwrite through normal interactions and can broadly influence model outputs even when prompts are phrased differently

lessons.point: Filter at the memory layer, not the prompt layer: memory-level filtering reached a 100% detection rate on the authors' own evaluated set of risky memory entries (not validated against unseen or adversarial memory content) and restored the no-memory baseline, while prompt-level fixes cost up to 15.9 pp of functional correctness.
lessons.excerpt: memory-level safety filtering achieves a 100\% detection rate on our evaluated risky memory entries and restores generation behavior to the without-memory baseline

entries.threat: An attacker or careless user plants an insecure preference in an LLM's long-term memory, durably degrading the security of code it later generates.
entries.conditions: Any coding assistant with cross-session persistent memory that stores user preferences without validating them.
entries.mitigations: Screen entries at write time to memory; treat stored preferences as untrusted input on read; prefer memory-level filtering over appending security requirements to prompts, which costs correctness.
entries.takeaway: Validate what goes into LLM memory - a poisoned preference outlives the conversation and can't be argued away.

actionable.title: Screen memory writes, not just prompts
actionable.detail: Add a write-time classifier on any persisted user preference touching security-relevant behavior (crypto choices, auth handling, input validation, deserialization) and re-validate on read. Prefer this to appending security boilerplate to every prompt, which the paper measures as costing up to 15.9 pp functional correctness.

entries.prior_art: Memory and context poisoning is an active area and RAG poisoning is well studied; this extends known memory-poisoning concepts rather than introducing a new primitive. The delta is a quantified measurement specific to stored *coding preferences* plus the risk-warning gap and the memory-vs-prompt mitigation comparison. The authors describe it as the first systematic study of this - their own characterization, not independently verified. Accepted to ISSTA 2026.
entries.verify_note: Independent verifier: dropped the unsupported 'agent' framing (source is LLM code generation with memory); scoped the 100% detection rate to the authors' own evaluated entries; attributed the 'first study' claim; novelty 76->50.

entries.title: Apple fixed the macOS Terminal ANSI DNS-exfiltration sink used to chain prompt injection
entries.author: Johann Rehberger
entries.summary: A prompt injection hidden in a spreadsheet cell made an LLM CLI tool emit ANSI escape codes that macOS Terminal interpreted as a DNS request, exfiltrating data from other rows with no tool or shell call involved. Reported to Apple in December 2024 and fixed in macOS Tahoe 26.1 (3 November 2025). The flaw spans two rendering-layer components, neither of them the model: the terminal emulator's escape-sequence handling (now patched) and CLI tools that pass model output to the terminal unencoded (still unaddressed industry-wide).

lessons.point: Rendering model output is itself a sink - data can leave without the agent ever invoking a tool, shell command, or MCP call.
lessons.excerpt: An attacker does not necessarily need to trigger a function, shell command, skill, or MCP tool to cause data leakage or other harm.

lessons.point: LLM-integrated CLIs should encode control characters by default (caret notation, like `cat -v`) and make raw terminal output an explicit opt-in.
lessons.excerpt: CLI tools should safely encode control characters by default, using an approach similar to `cat -v`. Raw terminal output should require explicit opt-in.

lessons.point: The injection tolerated an author error (BEL described as ASCII 10 rather than 7) and still worked - in-context instructions degrade gracefully, so near-miss payloads are not safe.
lessons.excerpt: That was my mistake in the prompt, but it did not prevent the in-context learning instruction from working.

entries.threat: Untrusted content processed by an LLM CLI causes the model to emit ANSI escape sequences that the terminal emulator acts on, exfiltrating data over DNS.
entries.conditions: An LLM-integrated CLI that passes model output to the terminal unencoded, on a terminal emulator that acts on escape sequences (macOS Terminal before Tahoe 26.1).
entries.mitigations: Update to macOS Tahoe 26.1+ for the emulator half; independently, encode control characters in your CLI's output by default - the author flags this as the still-unfixed half. Treat model output as untrusted data at every rendering boundary.
entries.takeaway: Sanitize model output at the rendering boundary - both the terminal emulator and your CLI's own output path are execution surfaces, and only the former got patched.

actionable.title: Encode control characters in LLM CLI output by default
actionable.detail: Wrap terminal writes in a caret-notation encoder so ESC/BEL and other control bytes cannot reach the emulator; expose raw output only behind an explicit flag. Reference implementations are linked from the post (Python dillma.py, Go terminalfriendly).

entries.prior_art: Every primitive was already established: the ANSI-DNS behavior by David Leadbeater (2023), LLMs emitting ANSI escapes by Leon Derczynski, and the author's own Terminal DiLLMa research (2024). This is disclosure-closure - the marginal contribution is the full chained PoC and the resulting Apple fix, not a new technique.
entries.verify_note: Independent verifier: the flaw spans two rendering components (terminal emulator, patched; CLI output encoding, unpatched), not one sink; prior-art attribution confirmed accurate; novelty 48->40.

entries.title: Self-state attacks: corrupting an agent's own memory and config uses legitimate syscalls
entries.author: Yimeng Chen et al.
entries.summary: Self-hosted agents read and write their own memory and configuration files, so once an attacker can write to that state the corruption itself is performed with legitimate OS system calls - the malicious step does not look anomalous at the OS layer. The authors formalize a four-axis attack space, realize 43 operations on real self-state files injected into traces from a representative self-hosted agent, and find a layered defense covers most cells with a small residual that stays structurally indistinguishable. Note the precondition: this assumes the attacker already has local write access; the paper does not address how that access is obtained.

lessons.point: Given an attacker who can already write to an agent's state files, the corruption step is performed with legitimate system calls - so the malicious action itself carries no distinguishing OS-level signal. This is a post-compromise finding, not a new access path.
lessons.excerpt: An agent may get compromised via corruption of its own state -- a compromise realized via legitimate OS system call invocation.

lessons.point: Layer the defense by state type: access-control prevention on instruction and configuration layers, workload-conditioned detection on the memory layer, periodic backup for recovery.
lessons.excerpt: a layered defense stack (access-control prevention on the instruction and configuration layers, workload-conditioned detection on the memory layer, and periodic backup for recovery) is effective on most attack cells

lessons.point: For a small residual subset of attack cells, OS-level signals alone are structurally insufficient to separate malicious from legitimate state changes - even though the layered stack handles most cells. Plan for recovery, not prevention alone.
lessons.excerpt: a small residual attack surface remains structurally indistinguishable at the OS level

entries.threat: An attacker with write access to an agent's instruction, configuration, or memory files redirects its behavior persistently, without exploiting the agent or the OS.
entries.conditions: Self-hosted agents persisting memory and configuration to the local filesystem, AND an attacker who has already obtained local write access to those files (e.g. via prior code execution).
entries.mitigations: Apply mandatory access control to instruction/config files, use workload-conditioned detection on memory files, and take periodic backups so recovery is possible for the undetectable residual.
entries.takeaway: Treat an agent's memory and config files as protected assets with their own access-control and backup policy - once an attacker can write them, the corruption step itself looks legitimate.

actionable.title: Put MAC + backup on agent instruction/config/memory files
actionable.detail: For self-hosted agents, separate the agent's write path from its instruction and configuration files (read-only mounts or MAC policy), apply anomaly detection conditioned on the agent's workload profile to the memory layer, and schedule periodic state backups so the structurally-undetectable cells remain recoverable.

entries.prior_art: Agent memory poisoning and config tampering have been discussed piecemeal. The contribution is systematizing the space (four-axis, 23-cell matrix) and measuring which cells OS defenses cover - not a new attack capability, since it presumes existing local write access. 'Self-state attacks' is the authors' own coinage in this paper. Preprint, not peer-reviewed.
entries.verify_note: Independent verifier: surfaced the unstated precondition - the attacker must already hold local write access, making this post-compromise systematization rather than a new access path; 'representative' agent not 'real'; novelty 73->45.

entries.title: Treat MCP tool descriptions as system prompts: silent re-trust poisoning
entries.summary: Once agents move from reading to acting, a poisoned MCP tool description turns prompt injection into an action. An approved third-party tool's description is silently modified to tell the agent to collect and exfiltrate data; because metadata updates don't re-trigger approval, the poisoned instructions go live with no review, and every individual agent action looks legitimate.

lessons.point: MCP mixes instructions and data in the same channel, so editing a tool's description reprograms the agent as effectively as editing its system prompt.
lessons.excerpt: The MCP blends instructions (tool descriptions) with data, so a change to a tool's metadata can redirect the agent's behavior as effectively as a change to its system prompt.

lessons.point: The dangerous gap is 'silent re-trust' (Microsoft's term): in configurations where a tool's description change doesn't re-trigger the approval workflow, poisoned instructions become active without review.
lessons.excerpt: In configurations where description changes do not trigger a re-approval workflow, the updated instructions become active without additional review.

lessons.point: The vulnerability is the trust boundary between systems, not any one system — each action (approved tool, inherited permissions, allowlisted endpoint) is individually legitimate.
lessons.excerpt: The vulnerability is not in any single system; it is in the trust boundary between them

entries.threat: An upstream tool maintainer modifies a tool description to redirect an agent into collecting and exfiltrating data, with no prompt, credential, or user action involved.
entries.mitigations: Change-review tool descriptions like system prompts; require re-approval on metadata changes; disable 'Allow all' tools; human-in-the-loop for high-impact actions; apply least agency, not just least privilege.
entries.takeaway: Version and change-review every MCP tool description as if it were a system prompt, and force re-approval whenever tool metadata changes.

actionable.title: Gate MCP tool-metadata changes behind re-approval
actionable.detail: Pin tool-description hashes for production agents and require an explicit re-approval (and imperative-language scan) whenever a server's tool description changes — do not let dynamic metadata updates silently re-trust.

entries.prior_art: Invariant Labs' April 2025 MCP tool-poisoning disclosure and OWASP's ASI02/ASI04 categories; this piece is a practical defender playbook on that known class, with the 'silent re-trust' on dynamic metadata as the sharpest incremental point.
entries.verify_note: Independent verifier: derivative Microsoft playbook on the known Invariant-Labs/OWASP tool-poisoning class; novelty lowered — held for review.
